title-group.article-title: Systematic Review: Porphyromonas gingivalis Outer Membrane Vesicles From Pathogenesis to Therapeutic Implications

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Outer membrane vesicles (OMVs) from Gram-negative bacteria are spherical lipid bilayer nanostructures with diameters of 20 to 250 nm. Porphyromonas gingivalis (P. gingivalis), a keystone periodontal pathogen, releases OMVs that mediate its virulence and systemic pathological effects.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This systematic review followed PRISMA guidelines and included studies from PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science up to 2025.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: A total of 62 articles were incorporated into the analysis. Generally, OMVs of P. gingivalis play a role in mediating interbacterial communication and are involved in pathogen-host interactions. These processes contribute to periodontal tissue destruction and systemic dissemination via blood vessels, thereby being associated with multiple systemic diseases. Key findings are as follows: (1) Periodontal destruction: OMVs inhibit endothelial cells (ECs)-mediated osteogenesis via the cGAS-STING-TBK1 pathway and induce apoptosis in human periodontal ligament stem cells (hPDLSCs) through the msRNA45033-CBX5-p53 methylation axis. Moreover, OMVs induce ferroptosis in BMSCs via the Hippo-YAP pathway. (2) Oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) progression: OMVs promote the development of OSCC by inducing NF-κB-regulated ferroptosis. Additionally, sRNA23392 within OMVs downregulates desmocollin-2, which impairs the invasion and migration of OSCC cells. (3) Systemic dissemination: OMVs can cross the blood-brain barrier (BBB) to transport bacterial components to distant organs, which is associated with Alzheimer's disease, cardiovascular disease, and diabetes mellitus. (4) Immune modulation: OMVs interact with macrophages and dendritic cells to trigger robust immune responses, inducing the secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: P. gingivalis OMVs act as key mediators of virulence dissemination and significantly modulate host-microbe interactions along the oral-systemic axis. This systematic review consolidates recent advancements in the research on P. gingivalis OMVs, emphasizing their roles in immunoregulation, pathogenic mechanisms, and associations with both periodontal and systemic diseases. Gaps in existing literature include strain-specific heterogeneity of OMVs and the dose-response relationships within systemic disease contexts. Future studies should employ multi-omics approaches and standardized methodologies to elucidate their heterogeneity and dose-response relationships, facilitating targeted therapies.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Periodontitis is a prevalent chronic inflammatory disease affecting over 50% of adults worldwide, leading to progressive destruction of periodontal tissues such as the gingiva, periodontal ligament, and alveolar bone.1 Although originating as a localized infection, its impact extends beyond the oral cavity, with strong associations linked to systemic conditions including cardiovascular disease, diabetes, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) and rheumatoid arthritis.2,3 The substantial global burden of periodontitis highlights its significance as a public health issue, warranting further research into its pathogenesis and systemic connections.4 The oral cell microenvironment and oral microorganisms form a complex ecosystem. The disruption of the balance of oral hygiene can lead to periodontitis.5,6 Among oral microorganisms, Porphyromonas gingivalis (P. gingivalis), a Gram-negative, obligate anaerobic bacterium, possesses a range of virulence factors that enable it to evade host immunity, disrupt tissue homeostasis, and reshape microbial communities, thereby driving both local and systemic pathology.7

Outer membrane vesicles (OMVs) are spherical, bilayered nanostructures (50-250 nm) released via outer membrane blebbing in Gram‑negative bacteria.8 As a key virulence determinant, OMVs carry a complex cargo of proteins, lipids, nucleic acids, and other biomolecules from the bacterial envelope and periplasmic space. Once regarded as mere byproducts, OMVs are now recognized as sophisticated vehicles that disseminate virulence factors, mediate interbacterial communication, and modulate host immune responses. In the periodontal pathogen, P. gingivalis, OMVs function as protected delivery systems that transport virulence factors to host cells while shielding them from degradation.9

P. gingivalis OMVs originate from the bacterial outer membrane and exhibit an asymmetric lipid bilayer composed of phospholipids in the inner leaflet and lipopolysaccharide (LPS) in the outer leaflet. Unlike typical enterobacterial LPS, P. gingivalis LPS features a unique C4′-monophosphoryl lipid A (C4′-MPLA) structure that elicits attenuated immunostimulatory activity via Toll-like receptor 4 (TLR4), potentially enabling immune evasion. The vesicle lumen contains periplasmic constituents and selectively packaged virulence factors, most notably gingipains (RgpA, RgpB, Kgp).10 Lipid A remodeling and sphingolipid (SL) synthesis are essential for OMV biogenesis, cargo sorting, and modulation of host immune responses. C4′-dephosphorylation of lipid A is required for normal OMV formation; OMVs containing penta-acylated lipid A strongly activate TLR4, and OMV abundance is inversely correlated with bacterial biofilm density.11 P. gingivalis-derived SLs are transferred to host macrophages via OMVs in a contact-independent fashion. SL deficiency modifies OMVs protein cargo and triggers excessive inflammatory responses, while OMVs carrying SLs efficiently suppress host immune activation.12 Furthermore, the capsule represents another key virulence factor of P. gingivalis that contributes to pathogenicity through multiple mechanisms, including attenuating inflammatory responses, escaping immune phagocytosis, and promoting bacterial survival.13

Research over the past decade has revealed that OMVs are not merely passive byproducts of bacterial growth but rather play active roles in pathogenesis. For P. gingivalis, OMVs serve multiple functions, including: (1) delivery of virulence factors to host cells; (2) modulation of host immune responses; (3) facilitation of biofilm formation; and (4) mediation of interspecies communication within the oral microbiome. Their stability and ability to penetrate host tissues make them particularly effective as long-distance virulence vehicles.

Perhaps most intriguingly, these nanostructures may serve as critical bridges connecting periodontal inflammation to systemic health conditions. Mounting evidence suggests that P. gingivalis OMVs can disseminate from the oral cavity to distant organs and tissues, where they may contribute to the pathogenesis of various diseases, including atherosclerosis, Alzheimer's disease, rheumatoid arthritis, and adverse pregnancy outcomes.14, 15, 16 The mechanisms underlying these systemic effects are multifaceted, involving direct tissue damage, amplification of inflammatory responses, and modulation of cellular functions.

Despite the growing body of research on P. gingivalis OMVs focused on exploring specific aspects of OMVs' biology (eg, biogenesis, cargo, or interaction with a single cell type), there is a lack of a comprehensive synthesis of evidence linking OMVs to the full spectrum of P. gingivalis-associated diseases. To the best of our knowledge, current reviews on bacterial OMVs either include P. gingivalis merely as a peripheral example or focus primarily on OMVs from other well‑studied pathogens (eg, Escherichia coli, Pseudomonas aeruginosa).10 This gap limits the ability of researchers and clinicians to integrate findings across studies, identify consistent mechanisms, and prioritize future research directions. This systematic review aims to collect, analyze, and synthesize the available knowledge from recent literature on P. gingivalis OMVs. By doing so, it seeks to provide a more complete picture of their enrichment and isolation methods, virulence components, mode of action, and relationship with periodontal disease and associated systemic diseases, which may in turn guide the development of novel prevention and treatment strategies and provide a foundational resource for advancing our understanding of P. gingivalis OMVs and their role in oral and systemic health.

sec.title: Development of the protocol
sec.p:
The reporting of this systematic review was guided by the 2020 updated PRISMA guidelines for systematic reviews.17 The PICOS framework was employed to answer the research question as stated below (Table 1):Table 1
PICOS framework for study eligibility criteria.Table 1 dummy alt text
PICOSInclusion criteriaExclusion criteria
PopulationIn vitro models: cell lines or primary cellsIn vivo models: animal modelsStudies not involving in vitro or vivo animal modelsClinical studies
InterventionPorphyromonas gingivalis-derived OMVsStudies not specifically focused on P. gingivalis OMVs (eg, OMVs from other bacteria, general studies on bacterial vesicles)
ComparisonAmong different concentrations of P. gingivalis OMVs and between the treated and control groups-
OutcomesAny outcome related to OMVs' biogenesis, composition, function (virulence delivery, immunomodulation, tissue disruption), or host interaction.Any outcome demonstrating a mechanistic link or association between P. gingivalis OMVs and periodontal/systemic diseases.Publications that could not provide substantial data to support outcomes (eg, conference abstracts, commentaries without original data)
Study designExperimental studies (in vitro or in vivo animal models)Case reports, conference abstracts, editorials, commentaries, narrative reviews, systematic reviews with or without meta-analysis, and non-comparative studies

sec.title: Inclusion criteria
sec.p: Studies were included if they met the following criteria: (1) Original research studies (including in vitro experiments and in vivo animal models) focused on P. gingivalis outer membrane vesicles (OMVs). (2) The studies provide substantial data available for extraction and analysis. (3) The studies must be published in peer-reviewed journals and written in English. (4) The studies must include both the treated and control groups.

sec.title: Exclusion criteria
sec.p: Studies were excluded based on the following criteria: (1) subject relevance, wherein all literature not specifically focused on P. gingivalis OMVs was eliminated. (2) methodological rigor, excluding reports that failed to clearly describe their outer membrane vesicle isolation protocols. (3) strength of evidence, discarding publications that could not provide substantial data support (eg, review articles, conference abstracts, case reports, and commentary articles. (4) Clinical studies. (5) Non-English studies were excluded from this review.

sec.title: Search strategy
sec.p: To comprehensively gather relevant evidence, a systematic literature search was conducted in the electronic databases of PubMed, Web of Science, and Scopus. The search was restricted to English-language articles published up to 2025. The following search keywords were used: ‘Porphyromonas gingivalis’ OR ‘P. gingivalis’ OR ‘Pg’ AND ‘outer membrane vesicles’ OR ‘OMVs’, OR ‘OMV’.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: Data extraction was performed independently by three reviewers. Any discrepancies were resolved through discussion until a consensus was reached; conflicts were resolved following discussion with a fourth investigator. The following data were extracted: (1) Author(s) and Year; (2) OMVs-Isolating Strain; (3) OMVs Preparation: OMVs Isolation Method (centrifugation, filtration, precipitation and affinity isolation); (4) OMVs-Treated Model (The specific type and identifier of cell lines or primary cells used (in vitro), or the animal strain, sex, and age (in vivo); (5) OMVs-Treated Method (In vitro: Co-culture conditions, stimulation concentration (dose), and duration of treatment. In vivo: Route of administration); (6) OMVs-Affected Molecule/Pathway (proteins, cytokines, immunoglobulins, signaling pathways); (7) Key Findings: the main findings of the study and their relevance to periodontal diseases or associated systemic diseases.

sec.title: Risk of bias and methodological quality assessment
sec.p: This study employed validated tools to conduct a rigorous risk of bias assessment for the included literature: The QUIN tool was used for 36 in vitro studies18 (Supplementary Table 1), consisting of 12 criteria scored as follows: adequately specified (2 points), inadequately specified (1 point), not specified (0 point), and not applicable (exclude criteria from calculation). The studies were classified by their final scores as high (<50%), medium (50% to 70%), or low risk (>70%) using the following formula: Final scores = (Total score × 100) / (number of criteria applicable × 2). The SYRCLE's risk of bias tool (Supplementary Table 2) was applied to 26 animal in vivo studies,19 covering 10 items related to selection, performance, detection attrition, reporting, and other bias. Each item was rated as ‘low’, ‘high’, or ‘unclear’ in all animal studies.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p:
The results of the literature search are shown in the following figure. A preliminary search identified 1019 papers, of which 213 were excluded due to duplicate records. Titles and abstracts of 805 publications were evaluated, and 741 were excluded. 64 publications were assessed by full-text reading, with 2 excluded based on inclusion criteria. Ultimately, 62 papers were included in the systematic review (Table 2).Table 2
The chart representing the screening process undertaken in this study.Table 2 dummy alt text
Image, table 2 dummy alt text

sec.title: Study characteristics
sec.p: This study employed a qualitative systematic review approach, in which existing evidence was summarized and analyzed via narrative synthesis to delineate key patterns and trends. An integrative qualitative analysis was then performed based on the synthesized findings from the included studies. Owing to substantial methodological heterogeneity across the included studies – including marked variations in P. gingivalis OMV preparation protocols (ultracentrifugation vs precipitation), experimental models (diverse cell lines and animal strains), intervention doses and durations, and outcome assessment metrics – quantitative data synthesis (ie, meta-analysis) was not feasible.

Methodological quality was systematically evaluated for the 26 included animal studies (Supplementary Table 2), revealing an overall evidence profile characterized by ‘rigorous outcome measurement yet limited transparency in experimental design’. Specifically, for measurement bias control (eg, baseline comparability, blinded outcome assessment), 95% of studies were rated as low risk, ensuring high internal validity for the observed outcomes (eg, inflammatory cytokine levels, histopathological scores). However, a large proportion of studies were judged to be at high or unclear risk in core experimental design domains – particularly random sequence generation and allocation concealment – representing the primary weakness in the current evidence chain of this field.

A total of 36 in vitro studies were included. Bias assessment showed that 2 studies (5.6%) were at low risk of bias, 33 (91.6%) at moderate risk, and 1 (2.8%) at high risk. The most prevalent methodological flaws across all studies included: insufficient blinding, lack of documentation for operators or outcome assessors, and absence of justification for sample size calculation or randomization procedures. These limitations are common in in vitro experimental research, suggesting that relevant findings should be interpreted with caution and that definitive conclusions should not be drawn from individual studies alone.

sec.title: The isolation and purification of P. gingivalis OMVs
sec.p:
P. gingivalis OMVs play a significant role in the progression of inflammatory diseases such as periodontitis and related systemic diseases. Therefore, successful OMVs isolation is a critical step in any meaningful investigation of their properties and applications. OMVs are typically isolated from bacterial culture supernatants based on their distinct physical or biochemical properties, so as to conduct accurate qualitative and quantitative analysis (Table 3).Table 3

sec.title: Centrifugation
sec.p: Ultracentrifugation has been the most commonly used method for the isolation and purification of P. gingivalis OMVs in recent years. The main principle is to set different centrifugal forces based on the size and density of different particles in the culture medium.77 Briefly, the culture medium of P. gingivalis was centrifuged at a low speed (10000 × g) to remove bacteria and debris for 10-15 min at 4°C, and the resulting supernatant was filtered with a pore size of 0.22-0.45 μm, and ultracentrifuged for 1-3 h at high centrifugal force (≥100,000 g) to isolate OMVs.21, 22, 26, 27, 30, 32, 37, 40, 41, 42, 44, 56, 59, 68, 69, 70, 71, 72, 73, 74, 76, 78 In this systematic review, most studies used ultracentrifugation for the enrichment and isolation of OMVs, which is a method that is simple to operate, thereby affording high-purity extracts (Table 3).

In addition, several studies employed density gradient centrifugation to further purify OMVs of P. gingivalis following an initial ultracentrifugation step. This technique realizes high-purity separation by exploiting differences in the particle size, molecular weight, and density between P. gingivalis OMVs and other biological molecules. However, it requires a specific concentration of density gradient media, such as iodixanol and sucrose.25,45 Hence, though this method yields a higher degree of purity in separation, it is more complex to operate and time-consuming.

sec.title: Filtration
sec.p: The ultrafiltration method is a technology that uses pressure as the driving force to selectively filter nanometer-sized substances according to the molecular size and morphology.79 This method is commonly employed in combination with ultracentrifugation. Briefly, the supernatant was filtered with a 0.2 to 0.45 μm filter after removing bacteria and debris, and then concentrated using an Ultra-15 Centrifugal Filter Device for a nominal molecular weight limit (NMWL) of 100,000.29, 31, 34, 39, 48, 49, 57, 58, 63, 66, 80 Then the concentrate will be mixed with the Total Exosome Isolation Reagent and incubated overnight at 4 °C. Afterward, the sample was centrifuged at 10000 × g for 60 minutes at 4 °C, and then the vesicles were eluted in PBS.29,31,39,49,54, 57, 58, 63, 66 Overall, this extraction process is relatively simple and minimally affects the biological activity of OMVs.

Tangential flow filtration using 100 kD filter capsules at 40 psi also enables the isolation of P. gingivalis OMVs, as demonstrated in a recent study. The principle of this method is to direct the fluid tangentially across the membrane, perpendicular to the direction of filtration. This gentle, parallel flow avoids subjecting the OMVs to vertical compression, thus safeguarding their membrane integrity.81

sec.title: Precipitation
sec.p: This method makes use of the hydrophobic nature of the lipid membrane and the water-soluble polymers that competitively bind with water molecules, thereby precipitating the hydrophobic proteins and lipid molecules. OMVs are enriched in proteins, which are precipitated by using ammonium sulfate. Then, OMVs are collected by centrifugation. Briefly, the proteins precipitated by ammonium sulfate from the culture supernatant were collected by centrifugation, followed by dialysis against a 10 mM sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.4).53, 67, 75

sec.title: Affinity isolation
sec.p: The main principle of affinity isolation is that OMVs are captured based on their immunophenotype or the presence of specific ligands on their surface (such as antibodies, aptamers, and resin). This method has a fast and simple process, but it can be limited because of a lack of specific markers. Similarly, OMVs were isolated by low-speed centrifugation, followed by 0.45-μm-pore-size and 0.22-μm-pore-size filters. Then the supernatant was poured into the separation column with binding resin. After the column was washed three times with binding buffer, P. gingivalis OMVs were eluted with OMVs elution buffer, collected, and stored at −80 °C.65

sec.title: Biogenesis of P. gingivalis OMVs and regulation of biofilm formation
sec.p: P. gingivalis OMVs act as a selectively packaged, independently delivered, and multifunctionally coordinated virulence effector complex. A 2014 study identified 151 proteins within P. gingivalis OMVs and revealed substantial, specific enrichment of virulence factors within these vesicles, particularly proteins harboring the C-terminal domain (CTD).76 Such vesicles encapsulate a variety of well-characterized virulence factors, including gingipains (Rgps and Kgp), fimbrial proteins (FimA and FimD), hemagglutinin (HagA), carboxypeptidase (CPG70), and arginine deiminase (PAD).73 Functionally, OMVs dose‑dependently inhibit the proliferation of human gingival fibroblasts (HGFs) and human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs), and strongly suppress endothelial capillary‑like tube formation in vitro.67 In addition, these vesicles exert potent platelet-aggregation-inducing activity, which is independent of bacterial fimbriae.74

Gram-negative bacteria usually evade the immune system detection by modifying the lipid A (the immunostimulatory component of LPS). For example, by removing the lipid A 4′-phosphate, P. gingivalis evades the induction of proinflammatory cytokine responses and ablates its ability to stimulate beta interferon (IFN-β) production.82 However, researchers determined that P. gingivalis OMVs are enriched for C4′-monophosphoryl lipid A (C4′-MPLA), which is an established agonist for TLR4-TRIF-IFNβ in HEK cells and inflammasome-IL-1β responses in THP-1 cells.10 In addition, a recent study demonstrated that P. gingivalis strains deficient in lipid A lpxF (C4′-phosphate) or carrying inactivating point mutations in the LpxF active site showed markedly reduced OMV production and higher biofilm density relative to the wild-type (WT) strain.10 These results suggest that the C4′-phosphate group of lipid A modulates both OMV biogenesis and biofilm formation in P. gingivalis. A functionally intact type IX secretion system (T9SS) is also an essential prerequisite for the activity of the outer membrane lipid A 1-phosphatase LpxE (which contains a unique C-terminal extension) and for normal OMV production in P. gingivalis. The T9SS may regulate LpxE activity by secreting specific cargo proteins; deletion of T9SS-related genes or lpxE leads to enlarged and deformed OMVs, impaired lipid A dephosphorylation, and altered OMV protein composition. This mechanism may be widely conserved among oral and gut Bacteroidetes.83

Recently, it has been demonstrated that Lipoprotein PG1881 is a critical component of the extracellular OMVs matrix that mediates cell-to-cell aggregation and matrix formation during subsurface growth, specifically promoting the binding of OMVs to the surface of RBCs.56 This is conducive to obtaining nutrients from the oral cavity, as well as the spread and survival of P. gingivalis. Furthermore, studies have demonstrated that IgM antibodies in antiserum against Porphyromonas gingivalis exert specific effects by recognizing O-glycan-modified proteins (eg, PG1881) on the surface of OMVs, providing a theoretical basis for the development of O-glycan-based vaccines and OMV-targeted therapies.66

P. gingivalis OMVs induce Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) aggregation via gingipains- and PPAD-dependent manner in vitro, as well as promoting the staphylococcal internalization by human neutrophils with no detectable neutrophil killing.24 Furthermore, studies have shown that P. gingivalis OMVs mediate the coaggregation and piggybacking of Treponema denticola and Lachnoanaerobaculum saburreum.70 This may be an immune evasive strategy by enhancing the phagocytic activity of other bacteria, distracting the neutrophil's attention from P. gingivalis. In addition, P. gingivalis activation of Toll-like receptor 2 (TLR2) requires both peptidylarginine deiminase (PPAD) activity and fimbriae. Wild-type (not PPAD-deficient) fimbriae enhance proinflammatory responses and P. gingivalis E2 pathway activation in human gingival fibroblasts via activation of the NF-κB and MAP kinase-dependent signaling pathways, and type I FimA avails the enhanced TLR2 signaling.59 On the other hand, P. gingivalis OMVs decreased the auto-aggregation of F. nucleatum, and proteases in P. gingivalis OMVs suppressed the invasion of F. nucleatum into oral epithelial cells by reducing FadA and FomA.65 Studies have revealed that P. gingivalis OMVs enter epithelial cells via a lipid raft-dependent endocytic pathway. Once internalized, the proteolytic activity of gingipains (Rgp and Kgp) carried by OMVs degrades key host proteins such as the transferrin receptor (TfR), paxillin, and focal adhesion kinase (FAK), leading to cellular dysfunction.69 Furthermore, evidence indicates that this entry process is regulated by Rac1.68

P. gingivalis OMVs from late-log and stationary growth stages exhibit more significant periodontal pathogenicity than pre-log OMVs, including stronger induction of M1 macrophage polarization, inhibition of periodontal ligament stem cell functions, and severe periodontal damage in rats.48

sec.title: Immune responses mediated by P. gingivalis OMVs
sec.p: A study revealed that P. gingivalis OMVs interfered with the complement system through multiple mechanisms, such as proteases and LPS, thereby protecting themselves and other bacteria from host immune attack.71 P. gingivalis OMVs upregulated the expression of maturation markers on BMDCs (CD11c+ Bone Marrow-Derived Dendritic Cells), including MHC class II, CD80, CD86, and CD40, and released the proinflammatory cytokines IL-1β, IL-6, IL-23, and IL-12p70. At the same time, it can also induce the differentiation of naive CD4+ T cells co-cultured with BMDCs into Th17 cells, and this process is mainly influenced by the cytokine IL-6.45

In addition, it has been established that P. gingivalis OMVs can activate and specifically coat human neutrophils in vitro, then induce degranulation without neutrophil killing. This process is because OMVs-bound proteases known as gingipains can inhibit the activity of the granule-derived MPO and LL-37 of human neutrophils.25 This is an effective strategy to evade the immune response, thereby ensuring the survival of the bacteria and creating a chronic inflammatory niche.

It was demonstrated that P. gingivalis OMVs induced higher levels of TNFα, IL-12p70, IL-6, IL-10, IFNβ, and NO in macrophages than P. gingivalis. Moreover, they can activate caspase-1 in macrophages, resulting in the production of large amounts of IL-1β, IL-18, whereas P. gingivalis cannot.28 P. gingivalis OMVs may play a dual role in the immune responses of human macrophages in a gingipain-dependent manner. Although P. gingivalis OMVs stimulated the production of cytokines and chemokines in human macrophages, specific Arg-gingipain and Lys-gingipain inhibition induced significant increases in IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, MCP-1, and RANTES levels. In addition, TNF-α secretion decreased when gingipains were blocked.21 Moreover, Sphingolipids, SL-null P. gingivalis increased the expression of TLR2, unchanged TLR4, and elevated expression of the adaptor molecules MyD88 and TRIF (Toll/IL-1 receptor domain-containing adaptor-inducing beta interferon) in THP-1 macrophage-like cells, meanwhile, caused a strong inflammatory immune response which elevated IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α. It demonstrated that SL-containing OMVs limited the ability of the host cell to mount a robust immune response.80 P. gingivalis OMVs can induce and modulate the inflammatory response and the initiation of acquired immunity in endothelial cells.75

Therefore, the high immunogenicity of P. gingivalis OMVs is expected to be used as a candidate antigen for vaccines. Some studies have shown that after intranasal immunization with P. gingivalis OMVs and Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans (Aa) OMVs, mice orally challenged with P. gingivalis and Aa significantly decreased numbers of both microorganisms compared to mock-immunized mice.50 In mouse models, intranasal (IN) immunization with P. gingivalis OMVs and Treponema denticola (Td) OMVs strongly induced humoral immune responses against each respective pathogen.51 Intranasal administration of P. gingivalis OMVs significantly induced secretory IgA (s-IgA) in nasal washes and saliva, as well as IgG and IgA in serum.72 Table 4 summarizes the key molecules/pathways affected by P. gingivalis OMVs and their main mechanisms.Table 4

This modification serves as the core target for immune recognition.

sec.title: OMVs in the pathogenesis and progression of oral diseases
Periodontitis
sec.p:
OMVs are considered the most effective offensive weapon of P. gingivalis for its survival in the oral cavity and the induction of periodontitis. Research has shown that P. gingivalis OMVs exhibit higher invasive efficiency into human oral epithelial cells, gingival fibroblasts, and human umbilical endothelial cells (70%-90% uptake) compared to the bacteria themselves (20%-50% uptake)32 (Table 5). P. gingivalis OMVs can be endocytosed by endothelial cells (ECs), activating the cGAS-STING-TBK1 pathway, which has been reported to be triggered by endothelial dysfunction. This process impairs EC-mediated osteogenesis (eg, inhibiting MG63 migration/differentiation) and promotes alveolar bone resorption, with elevated STING levels in blood vessels and reduced Runx2+ osteoblast-like cells.22 In addition, P. gingivalis OMVs activate the Hippo-YAP signaling pathway, thus inducing ferroptosis to impede bone regeneration in bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells (BMSCs).47 Further study reveales that P. gingivalis OMVs unregulate the SAA3-mediated TLR4/MyD88/NF-κB p65 axis in the mouse BMSCs, thus reducing the expression of Alp, Runx2, and Ocn, which in turn inhibites osteogenic differentiation of P.g-OMVs-treated BMSCs.61Table 5

Lv et al32s025In vivo: 30 mg/kgIn vitro: 25 mg/mLLSKL, a specific inhibitor of Thbs1, significantly downregulates Thbs1, TGF-β1, TGF-βR2, SMAD2/3 expression in hepatic stellate cells, and inhibits P. gingivalis OMVs-mediated NAFLD progression. These findings identify the Thbs1/TGF-β pathway as a potential therapeutic target for NAFLD patients with comorbid periodontitis.NAFLD

P. gingivalis OMVs exacerbate periodontitis by inhibiting proliferation, promoting apoptosis, and suppressing osteogenic differentiation of periodontal ligament stem cells (PDLSCs) in vitro and in rat models; And pretreatment with 4-phenylbutyric acid (4-PBA) blocks OMVs-induced PERK pathway activation and restores PDLSC osteogenesis, indicating involvement of endoplasmic reticulum dysfunction.34 Further research reveals sRNA45033 in P. gingivalis OMVs targets CBX5, which regulates the methylation of p53 DNA. That is associated with apoptosis in hPDLC.26

P. gingivalis OMVs promote human gingival fibroblasts (HGFs) senescence by the upregulation of IL21R-AS1, which acts as a ceRNA, mediating the miR-500a-3p/FBXW7 axis. The specific mechanism might be P. gingivalis OMVs activating TLR4 and increasing PAX5 expression, leading to elevated IL21R-AS1 levels.61,43

In addition, human gingival epithelial cells (OBA-9) co-cultured with P. gingivalis OMVs induce IL-6 and IL-8 production via activation of Erk1/2, JNK, p38 MAPK, NF-κB, and STING signaling pathways, contributing to periodontitis exacerbation.58 Studies have identified that TLR2 signaling as the sole core pathway mediating osteoclast differentiation and activation is induced by oral bacteria‑derived extracellular vesicles (including OMVs). These vesicles bind to TLR2 on osteoclast precursors and trigger downstream signaling, which markedly upregulates proinflammatory cytokines associated with osteoclastogenesis (eg, TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β, G-CSF). This cascade further promotes the differentiation of osteoclast precursors into mature TRAP-positive osteoclasts, ultimately leading to bone resorption and alveolar bone loss.84

However, A study indicated that curcumin protected human gingival epithelial (HGE) cells from the cytotoxic effects of P. gingivalis OMVs by inhibiting the expression of IL-6, IL-1β, and TNF-α. Moreover, curcumin inhibited the cytotoxic effects of OMVs on cellular migration and OMVs' adherence to and entry into cells, as well as cellular apoptotic death.36 Moreover, a study reported that Stannous fluoride (SnF2), stannous chloride (SnCl2), and SnF2-containing toothpaste protect gingival keratinocytes from P. gingivalis OMVs-induced infection and oxidative stress by precipitating OMVs/LPS into insoluble aggregates (via 1:1 stannous-LPS binding).60

sec.title: Oral squamous cell carcinoma, OSCC
sec.p: Studies have shown that P. gingivalis OMVs have been proven to increase the risk of oral cancers and the proliferation and migration of OSCC. Recently, a study revealed that P. gingivalis OMVs may be an indicator of poor prognosis for OSCC through downregulating the expression of TNFSF15, ZNF292, ATRX, ASPM, and KIF20B.64 Further research showed that sRNA23392 was abundant in P. gingivalis OMVs, and it can target and degrade DSC2 (desmocollin-2) and reduce the expression of DSC2, which affects the invasion and migration ability of OSCC cells.46 Additionally, P. gingivalis OMVs can inhibit ferroptosis of OSCC by activating NF-κB signaling to induce the EMT of OSCC cells,44 which promotes OSCC progression. Moreover, one study showed that P. gingivalis OMVs hampered the activation of the cGAS-STING pathway by suppressing the interaction of STING–IRF3 and TBK1–IRF3, and impeded pIRF3′s nuclear translocation, subsequently decreasing the expression of IFN-β in vitro and in vivo models of OSCC. P. gingivalis OMVs were also found to decrease the infiltration of NK cells and dendritic cells (DCs) into mouse tumors.85 Overall, the invasion of P. gingivalis OMVs not only compromises the host's innate immunity but also promotes cancer immune evasion to facilitate tumor growth.

sec.title: OMVs in the pathogenesis and progression of systemic diseases
sec.p: Research has shown that P. gingivalis OMVs can enter the blood and spread to and affect distant tissues and organs. Therefore, P. gingivalis OMVs not only play a crucial role in the pathogenesis and progression of oral diseases but also have an important impact on systemic diseases.

sec.title: Alzheimer's disease, AD
sec.p: Previous research has shown that P. gingivalis and its virulence factors may influence the pathogenesis of AD. But viable P. gingivalis has not been isolated from human brain tissues.35 In situ Raman spectroscopy reveals that P. gingivalis OMVs (containing LPS and gingipains) induce metabolic alterations in human neuroblastoma (SH-SY5Y) cells, associated with AD-related pathologies like amyloid β (Aβ) plaque formation, Tau hyperphosphorylation, and cholesterol agglomeration.53 P. gingivalis OMVs, including gingipains, can reach the cerebral ventricle in BALB/cAJc1 mice and induce neuroinflammation by activating microglia. Tau and phosphorylated Tau protein increased.63

Following the endocytosis of P. gingivalis-OMVs, can decrease the expression of neurite markers GAP43 and Syn in SH-SY5Y cells (Human neuroblastoma cells).23 Furthermore, the P. gingivalis-OMVs-associated LPS can induce an increase in iNOS and TNF-α levels via AKT and JNK signaling pathways in BV-2 cells (Mouse microglial cells).23 Furthermore, P. gingivalis OMVs significantly increase GFAP and Iba1 signals in the hippocampus region of mouse brains, which represents glial activation, and they increase the protein expression levels of IL-1β, TNF-α, and IL-6 in the hippocampus.55

Notably, P. gingivalis OMVs are the direct contributors to induce BBB damage by disrupting tight junctions in brain endothelial cells, which facilitates their crossing of the BBB and subsequent induction of neuroinflammation.55 Previous studies have confirmed that P. gingivalis OMVs compromise the integrity of the human BBB tight junction in vitro model. The specific mechanism might be electrical resistance decreasing, recovery deficit, and disrupted zona occludens protein (ZO-1) in brain microvascular endothelial cells.54 Further research reveals that P. gingivalis OMVs decreased tight junction–related gene expression of ZO-1, occludin, claudin-5, and occludin protein expression in the mice hippocampus.29

In fact, one study reported that intraperitoneal injection of P. gingivalis OMVs triggers time-dependent neuroinflammation and brain histopathological changes in adult zebrafish, including early increases in nitric oxide/P. Gingivalis E2, later IL-1β/IL-6 expression, and brain lesions (coagulation, astrocytosis, necrosis) between 6 and 24 hours.20

Additionally, the absence of CatB effectively alleviated P. gingivalis OMV-induced cognitive dysfunction, microglia-mediated neuroinflammation, tau hyperphosphorylation, and synapse loss by a SAPK/JNK-dependent manner in the mouse model.39 However, P. gingivalis OMVs can promote the synthesis and processing of pro-IL-1β through CatB/CatL-independent phagocytic mechanisms in BV-2 microglia (murine microglial cell line).35

sec.title: Adverse pregnancy outcomes
sec.p: A previous study reported that P. gingivalis OMVs facilitate neutrophil chemoattraction and activation in trophoblast-conditioned media, but inhibit endothelial cell migration and activation. It proved that P. gingivalis OMVs, during the early stages of pregnancy and placentation, disrupt the trophoblast-mediated regulation of vascular remodeling and immune homeostasis.41 Further study showed that P. gingivalis OMVs modulated HTR-8 (human first-trimester trophoblast) metabolism by reducing the expression of the glucose transporter GLUT1 and decreasing total reactive oxygen species with sustained mitochondrial activity, which might result in lower placental and fetal weight gain in vivo.40 Moreover, maternal exposure during pregnancy to P. gingivalis OMVs reduces embryonic mouse pup brain weight, decreases cortical neuron density (Cux1/SatB2 reduction), activates microglia (Iba-1 increase), and elevates Tau phosphorylation (Thr231), indicating OMVs disrupt fetal brain development.37

sec.title: Cardiovascular disease, CVD
sec.p: P. gingivalis OMVs upregulated the expression of runt-related transcription factor 2 (Runx2) through activation of extracellular-regulated kinase (ERK1/2), leading to vascular smooth muscle cell (VSMC) calcification both in vitro and in vivo.62 P. gingivalis OMVs increase vascular permeability in vitro (human microvascular endothelial cells) and in vivo (zebrafish larvae infection model), with OMV surface gingipains mediating these effects via proteolytic cleavage of endothelial adhesion molecules like PECAM-1 (CD31).27 Further research reveals that it might be because P. gingivalis OMVs increase vascular permeability by promoting stress fiber formation and lysosome/endosome-mediated vascular endothelial-cadherin (VEC) degradation in human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) and human pulmonary microvascular endothelial cells (HPMECs). Rho kinases (ROCK) may be the key factors that regulate both pathways.86 Moreover, a previous study has shown that the expression of endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS) was suppressed through the activation of ROCK-mediated ERK1/2 and p38 MAPK upon co-treatment of P. gingivalis OMVs with the mouse aorta endothelium, contributing to the dysfunction of endothelial cells.38

In the zebrafish larvae animal model, P. gingivalis OMVs induced cardiovascular injury, including pericardial enlargement, vascular damage, increased neutrophils, and activated inflammatory pathways, with transcriptomic data linking these effects to immune response and extracellular matrix (ECM)-receptor interaction pathways.30

sec.title: Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, NAFLD
sec.p: OMVs may contribute to NAFLD pathogenesis via systemic inflammation and oxidative stress.3 Experimental evidence confirmed that P. gingivalis OMVs promoted the activation of hepatic stellate cells and liver fibrosis by activating the Thbs1/TGF-β signaling pathway, offering key insights into the molecular mechanism of NAFLD.78 Furthermore, extracellular vesicle derived from periodontal pathogens induced hepatic steatosis through activation of the TLR2/PAI‑1 axis, revealing a potential mechanistic link between periodontal infections and NAFLD. PAI-1 levels are positively correlated with TLR2 activation, highlighting its crucial role in the progression of NAFLD.

sec.title: Other systemic diseases
sec.p: Researchers demonstrated that P. gingivalis OMVs aggravated renal dysfunction, renal tubulointerstitial lesions, and fibrosis in UUO (unilateral ureteral obstruction) mice. P. gingivalis OMVs upregulated gene and protein expression levels of EMT-related regulatory factors such as β-catenin and Snail1, and renal tubular epithelial cells (RTECs) may undergo epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT), leading to their transformation into myofibroblasts in the progression of renal interstitial fibrosis (RIF).42

Gingipains were internalized into intestinal cells through OMVs and transported into the cytosol, where they then directly degraded occludin from the cytosolic side. That increased the permeability of the Caco-2 cell (Human intestinal epithelial cell line).38

P. gingivalis OMVs triggered the activation of caspase-3 and cleavage of PARP, resulting in Human lung adenocarcinoma epithelial cell (A549) apoptosis, and disrupted tight junction proteins between cells.31

P. gingivalis OMVs, carrying gingipains, translocated to the mouse liver and increased blood glucose levels by attenuating insulin sensitivity. They also impaired hepatic insulin-induced Akt/glycogen synthase kinase-3 β (GSK-3β) signaling in a gingipain-dependent manner in hepatocytic cells, leading to decreased hepatic glycogen synthesis. This disruption of hepatic glucose metabolism may contribute to the progression of diabetes.57 More recently, a study has found that P. gingivalis OMVs exacerbate damage to the BBB in diabetic retinopathy (DR) mice. They enhance the inflammatory response in human retinal microvascular endothelial cells (HRMECs) and increase reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels due to mitochondrial dysfunction. Additionally, they induce mitochondrial-mediated cell death (MMCD) and increase endothelial cell permeability through PAR-2 activation in HRMECs.52

sec.title: Preparation of P. gingivalis OMVs
sec.p: Isolation and purification of P. gingivalis OMVs are fundamental procedures for investigating their pathogenic mechanisms and potential clinical applications. Current studies predominantly adopt multiple enrichment strategies based on the physical and biochemical characteristics of OMVs, including centrifugation, filtration, precipitation, and affinity isolation. In the extraction of P. gingivalis OMVs, ultracentrifugation has long served as the most extensively validated standard operating procedure (SOP),21, 22, 26, 27, 30, 32, 37, 40, 41, 42, 44, 56, 59, 77 owing to its favorable compatibility, operational simplicity, and capacity to enrich high-purity vesicle preparations. For studies requiring higher purity, density gradient centrifugation (eg, iodixanol or sucrose gradients) is commonly combined with ultracentrifugation to further remove non-vesicular components based on density differences. Although this approach is complex and time-consuming, it enables the isolation of ultra-pure OMVs.25,45

In addition, filtration methods, including low-speed centrifugation to remove debris, filtration through 0.22 to 0.45 μm membranes, and 100 kDa ultrafiltration, are frequently used in conjunction with centrifugation. These methods preserve the biological activity of OMVs and are suitable for large-scale preparation.29, 31, 34, 39, 48, 49, 54, 57, 58, 63, 66, 80, 81 Precipitation methods (eg, ammonium sulfate precipitation) and affinity isolation are adopted for cost control or rapid capture of specifically labeled vesicles, respectively, but are limited by compromised purity or specificity.53,65

Future technical optimization will likely focus on balancing separation efficiency and retention of vesicle bioactivity. For instance, the further development of mild and high-efficiency affinity capture systems is expected to achieve the dual goals of large-scale production and functional preservation of OMVs.

sec.title: Concentration of P. gingivalis OMVs
sec.p: Systematic analysis of the included studies reveals methodological heterogeneity as a prominent feature within this field. Specifically, in vitro concentrations varied by more than six orders of magnitude, ranging from 0.1 ng/mL to 100 μg/mL or higher. Substantial variability was also evident in the basis of OMV quantification (eg, mass, particle count, or multiplicity of infection), treatment duration (1-72 hours), and in vivo administration regimens.

This heterogeneity stems from several underlying factors. First, study objectives diverge fundamentally, ranging from investigations into physiological low-dose signaling to assessments of supraphysiological or toxicological effects. Second, a unified quantification paradigm is lacking. Although mass-based concentration constitutes the prevailing metric, it is afflicted by inherent variability; more rigorous metrics such as particle enumeration have not yet been widely adopted as standard practice. Finally, advances in experimental design epitomize an evolving scientific understanding: whereas early studies typically relied on single time-point analyses, contemporary methodologies increasingly employ multi-factorial dose–time matrix frameworks.

The fundamental divergence in quantification methodologies (mass vs particle count vs MOI) constitutes a major contributor to the lack of direct comparability between reported concentrations and presents a substantial barrier to experimental reproducibility. We emphatically advocate that future studies report particle count–based concentrations as a minimum reporting standard, alongside complementary metrics, to enable greater data harmonization and cross-study integration.

sec.title: Synthesis of key findings: P. gingivalis OMVs as central mediators of pathogenesis
sec.p:
The findings of this systematic review confirm that P. gingivalis OMVs are not passive byproducts of bacterial metabolism but active, multifunctional mediators of P. gingivalis's pathogenic effects – both locally in the periodontium and systemically. Three overarching themes emerge from the synthesized evidence: (1) The pathogenicity of OMVs is manifested in biogenesis and biofilm formation, which, along with their invasion of host cells and activation of immune cells, collectively trigger a host inflammatory response; (2) OMVs cargo is selectively enriched in virulence factors that act in concert to disrupt host homeostasis; and (3) OMVs facilitate P. gingivalis's systemic impact by crossing epithelial barriers and modulating distant cell types (Figure 1).Fig. 1
Mechanisms underlying Porphyromonas gingivalis-mediated oral and systemic diseases development. hPDLC: human periodontal ligament cells; HGE, human gingival epithelial cells; HGFs, human gingival fibroblasts; OSCC, oral squamous cell carcinoma; BMSCs, bone marrow mesenchymal stem cells; GLUT1, glucose transporter; TROS, oxygen species; RIF, renal interstitial fibrosis; EMT, epithelial-mesenchymal transition; PARP, poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase; ERK1/2, extracellular-regulated kinase; ROCK, Rho kinases; HRMECs, human retinal microvascular endothelial cells; DSC2, desmocollin-2; VSMC, vascular smooth muscle cell; DR, diabetic retinopathy.Fig 1 dummy alt text

First, regarding OMVs' biogenesis, P. gingivalis does not produce OMVs constitutively but in response to conditions that mimic the subgingival microenvironment. For example, iron limitation – a hallmark of the periodontal pocket, where iron is sequestered by host transferrins and lactoferrins – upregulates OMVs production by P. gingivalis.33 This is likely an adaptive strategy, as OMVs carry proteins involved in iron acquisition (eg, PG1881, a lipoprotein that mediates binding to red blood cells [RBCs] for heme extraction).87 Similarly, exposure to host inflammatory cytokines (eg, TNF-α) increases OMVs release, suggesting that P. gingivalis uses OMVs to counteract host immunity.88 The biogenesis process itself is regulated by multiple mechanisms, including the activity of lipid A remodeling enzymes (eg, LpxF, a C4′-phosphatase). Studies using LpxF-deficient P. gingivalis mutants demonstrated a 70% reduction in OMVs production compared to wild-type strains, confirming that lipid A dephosphorylation is essential for OMVs formation.13 This finding is particularly significant, as lipid A is also a key component of P. gingivalis LPS – a major OMVs cargo molecule – linking OMVs biogenesis to immune activation.

Second, the cargo of P. gingivalis OMVs is characterized by a high degree of selectivity and functional coordination. P. gingivalis OMVs comprise several vital virulence factors, including gingipain (Kgp, RgpA, and RgpB), LPS, hemagglutinin (HagA), heme-binding protein (HBP35), fimbrial adhesins (FimA, Mfa1), and peptidyl arginine deiminase (PPAD).89 Interestingly, small RNAs similar in size to eukaryotic microRNAs (miRNAs) were also observed in P. gingivalis OMVs, which can be delivered to eukaryotic cells. And these exogenous miRNAs could suppress the expression of certain cytokines in Jurkat T cells.90 Proteomic analyses of P. gingivalis OMVs have identified over 150 proteins, with ∼20% classified as virulence factors.90 Notably, gingipains (RgpA, RgpB, Kgp) are among the most abundant OMVs-associated proteins, and their activity is preserved within vesicles.91 This is critical because free gingipains are rapidly degraded by host proteases, whereas OMVs encapsulation protects them from inactivation. For example, OMV-associated RgpA retains its ability to degrade host immunoglobulins (eg, IgG) and cytokines (eg, IL-8), enabling P. gingivalis to evade adaptive immunity and modulate inflammation.91 Similarly, OMV-associated LPS – which has a unique lipid A structure (with fewer acyl chains and reduced phosphorylation compared to enterobacterial LPS) – activates TLR4 in a manner that favors pro-inflammatory cytokine production (eg, TNF-α) without inducing robust antibacterial responses.92 This ‘biased’ activation of TLR4 likely contributes to the chronic, low-grade inflammation that characterizes periodontitis and its systemic sequelae.

Third, the review underscores the ability of P. gingivalis OMVs to act as ‘long-range’ effectors. Unlike P. gingivalis itself – which is primarily confined to the subgingival biofilm – OMVs can diffuse through GCF, penetrate the gingival epithelium (via endocytosis by epithelial cells), and enter the systemic circulation.33 Once in the bloodstream, OMVs can interact with distant cell types, including vascular endothelial cells (contributing to CVD), adipocytes (contributing to T2DM), microglia (contributing to AD), and synovial fibroblasts (contributing to RA).93,92 For example, in a mouse model of AD, intravenous injection of P. gingivalis OMVs led to the accumulation of OMVs in the hippocampus, where gingipains cleaved tau protein (a key component of neurofibrillary tangles) and LPS activated microglia to produce neuroinflammatory cytokines (eg, IL-1β).8 This finding provides a direct mechanistic link between P. gingivalis OMVs and AD pathogenesis, supporting the ‘oral-brain axis’ hypothesis.

sec.title: Mechanistic insights into OMVs-mediated host cell disruption
sec.p: The interaction of P. gingivalis OMVs with host cells is a complex, multistep process that begins with OMVs attachment to the cell surface and ends with the delivery of cargo to the host cytoplasm. The review identifies several key mechanisms by which OMVs disrupt host cell function, with notable differences across cell types.

In oral epithelial cells (eg, gingival epithelial cells), OMVs attach via interactions between OMV-associated fimbriae (FimA) and host cell surface receptors (eg, β1 integrins).94 Following attachment, OMVs are internalized via clathrin-dependent endocytosis, and their cargo is released into the cytoplasm.94 Once inside, gingipains degrade tight junction proteins (occludin, JAM-A), disrupting the epithelial barrier and allowing P. gingivalis (and other subgingival bacteria) to invade underlying tissues.31 This barrier disruption is further exacerbated by OMVs-induced apoptosis of epithelial cells, mediated by the activation of caspase-3 and cleavage of PARP.31

In hPDLCs – critical for maintaining PDL integrity and alveolar bone homeostasis – OMVs exert their effects via a distinct mechanism involving small RNAs. Specifically, OMVs carry msRNA sRNA45033, which is internalized by hPDLCs and targets the host gene CBX5 (a component of the Polycomb repressive complex 1).95 Knockdown of CBX5 in hPDLCs recapitulates the apoptotic and pro-inflammatory effects of OMVs, while overexpression of CBX5 abrogates these effects.26 Further mechanistic studies using CUT&Tag (cleavage under targets and tagmentation) analysis revealed that CBX5 regulates apoptosis by methylating the promoter of p53, a tumor suppressor gene that drives cell death when activated.26 This finding is particularly significant because hPDLC apoptosis and reduced proliferation are key drivers of PDL degradation and alveolar bone resorption in periodontitis.

In immune cells, P. gingivalis OMVs employ a strategy of ‘immune evasion with inflammation’. In this review, P. gingivalis OMVs exhibit significant immunomodulatory effects. For instance, they can induce the maturation of dendritic cells, thereby promoting CD4+T cell differentiation. Additionally, OMVs stimulate macrophages to secrete inflammatory factors and enable the bacteria to evade neutrophil surveillance. For example, OMVs coat human neutrophils and induce degranulation, releasing antibacterial peptides (eg, LL-37) and enzymes (eg, myeloperoxidase [MPO]).45 However, OMV-associated gingipains degrade these antibacterial molecules, allowing P. gingivalis to survive while maintaining an inflamed environment.45 In monocytes, OMVs induce tolerance to subsequent P. gingivalis infection by activating TLR4 and mTOR-dependent pathways, leading to the selective abrogation of TNF-α production.95 This tolerance prevents a robust immune response to P. gingivalis, facilitating bacterial persistence in the periodontium.

sec.title: Implications for systemic disease: the OMVs-mediated ‘oral-systemic axis’
sec.p: Accumulating evidence has increasingly delineated a discrete ‘oral-systemic disease axis’, in which P. gingivalis and its released OMVs act as pivotal mediators, connecting local periodontal infection to the initiation and progression of multiple distal systemic disorders. Rather than targeting specific organs in isolation, OMVs exert catalytic effects across diverse pathological pathways through a suite of conserved pathogenic mechanisms – including immune modulation and inflammatory amplification, breaching of biological barriers, and direct perturbation of core cellular processes (Figure 1).

sec.title: Immune modulation and systemic inflammatory amplification
sec.p: This represents a core mechanism by which OMVs drive systemic pathology. Pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) harbored by OMVs, including LPS and gingipains, can broadly activate immune responses in diverse cell types via pattern recognition receptors such as TLR4. Within the cardiovascular system, OMVs bind to vascular endothelial cells, inducing the expression of adhesion molecules (ICAM-1, VCAM-1) and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α), thereby promoting monocyte recruitment to the vascular wall and facilitating the initiation of atherosclerosis.96 Similarly, in the central nervous system, OMVs-associated LPS activates microglia through TLR4, stimulating the release of neuroinflammatory factors (IL-1β, TNF-α) and reactive oxygen species that contribute to neuronal damage and loss – a hallmark pathological alteration in Alzheimer's disease (AD).55 P. gingivalis OMVs can be internalized by Kupffer cells (liver macrophages), prompting their activation and secretion of pro‑inflammatory cytokines (eg, IL-6, TNF-α), thereby directly fueling hepatic inflammation – a defining feature of NAFLD.3 Moreover, amphiphilic components of P. gingivalis OMVs can activate TLR2, leading to elevated expression of plasminogen activator inhibitor‑1 (PAI‑1), a key mediator of hepatic lipid accumulation and steatosis. Consistent with these findings, clinical studies have demonstrated a significant positive correlation between serum TLR2 activation potential and PAI‑1 levels in NAFLD patients.97 Collectively, this persistent low‑grade inflammation elicited by OMVs across multiple organ systems constitutes a unifying pathological basis that connects periodontitis to a spectrum of chronic systemic diseases.

sec.title: Penetration and disruption of biological barriers
sec.p: OMVs are capable of traversing and compromising key biological barriers, thereby driving the systemic dissemination of local inflammatory responses. They not only disseminate to distal organs via the bloodstream but also directly disrupt the structural integrity of biological barriers. In the setting of AD pathogenesis, OMVs have been demonstrated to disrupt tight junctions between brain microvascular endothelial cells, impairing BBB function and enabling the infiltration of pathogenic components into the brain parenchyma, which in turn exacerbates neuroinflammation and pathological progression.55 Similarly, at the vascular level, interactions between OMVs and endothelial cells may also compromise vascular endothelial barrier function. This intrinsic barrier-penetrating capacity allows pathogenic factors originally confined to the oral cavity to directly target and act upon distant organs.

sec.title: Direct interference with key cellular pathways and functions
sec.p: Beyond broad immune activation, OMVs can directly perturb core physiological functions of specific tissues via their cargo of active components, such as proteases. In the context of type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM), proteins associated with the gingipain Kgp carried on OMVs can specifically cleave the extracellular domain of the insulin receptor on adipocytes and hepatocytes. This direct proteolytic action impairs insulin binding capacity and its downstream signaling (eg, AKT phosphorylation), thereby directly exacerbating insulin resistance.10 In models of atherosclerosis, OMVs can be internalized by vascular smooth muscle cells (VSMCs), directly inducing their proliferation and migration and thus accelerating atherosclerotic plaque progression.98

These shared mechanisms converge in different disease contexts, leading to characteristic pathological outcomes. In a mouse model of atherosclerosis, intraperitoneal injection of P. gingivalis OMVs increased aortic plaque area by a significant 40%.98 In a T2DM (db/db) mouse model, oral administration of OMVs elevated fasting blood glucose by 25% and reduced insulin sensitivity by 30%.11 In an AD (5 × FAD) mouse model, intracerebroventricular injection of OMVs increased Aβ plaque burden by 35% and elevated tau protein phosphorylation levels by 50%.55 Collectively, these data firmly establish OMVs as direct effector molecules that link oral infection to the onset and progression of multiple systemic diseases.

sec.title: Therapeutic potential of targeting P. gingivalis OMVs: opportunities and challenges
sec.p: The findings of this review highlight several promising therapeutic strategies for targeting P. gingivalis OMVs, with applications in both periodontitis and its systemic sequelae. One potential approach is to develop strategies to inhibit OMVs' biogenesis. Targeting the factors involved in the physical force model, disruption of the outer membrane - peptidoglycan linkage, or the response to extracellular signals, could potentially reduce OMVs production. Another approach could be to design drugs or agents that specifically target the virulence factors carried by OMVs, such as inhibitors of gingipains. In addition, OMVs-based vaccines could elicit protective immunity against multiple virulence factors simultaneously, potentially providing broad protection against P. gingivalis pathogenesis. However, there are also challenges. The complex and dynamic nature of OMVs, along with the ability of P. gingivalis to adapt to different environments, may pose difficulties in developing effective therapies. Additionally, ensuring the specificity of the therapeutic agents to target OMVs and P. gingivalis without affecting normal oral microbiota and host cells is a significant challenge.

sec.title: Limitations of existing evidence and future directions
sec.p: While the synthesized results provide a comprehensive framework for understanding P. gingivalis OMVs' role in periodontitis and systemic disease, several critical limitations in the existing evidence must be acknowledged. A major limitation of the current literature is the overreliance on simplified in vitro systems and rodent models that poorly recapitulate the complexity of human periodontal disease and systemic physiology. By bridging this gap – through more relevant models, characterization of OMVs subpopulations, and human studies – future research can unlock the full potential of P. gingivalis OMVs as diagnostic biomarkers and therapeutic targets, ultimately improving oral and systemic health outcomes for periodontitis patients. An addition key limitation of this review is the deliberate exclusion of clinical studies, which may diminish its translational relevance and weaken the bridge between preclinical mechanistic insights and clinical application. We adopted this focused approach intentionally, primarily because the existing literature lacks standardized methodologies for OMVs research. An additional key consideration is that this review centers on fundamental preclinical and mechanistic evidence regarding OMVs biogenesis, cargo, and function, whereas clinical studies address translational questions that require distinct inclusion criteria and assessment frameworks. Nevertheless, clinical studies on OMVs are highly meaningful and will be explored in depth in our future work.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This review synthesizes the recent literature to summarize commonly used enrichment and isolation techniques for P. gingivalis OMVs, as well as their biogenesis, regulation, and role of OMVs in biofilm formation, immunomodulatory functions, and pathogenic mechanisms in oral and associated systemic diseases. This facilitates a deeper understanding of OMVs' composition and pathogenic mechanisms, thereby paving the way for their considerable biotechnological potential, notably as tools for diagnosis and targeted therapy. P. gingivalis OMVs play a multifaceted and crucial role in the pathogenesis of periodontal disease and are associated with various systemic diseases. Their biogenesis, composition, and interactions with host cells contribute to a complex network of events that lead to tissue destruction and immune dysregulation. Understanding these processes yields valuable insights into the development of periodontal disease and related systemic conditions. While there are challenges, the knowledge gained also offers potential therapeutic targets. Future research efforts should focus on addressing existing knowledge gaps to advance our understanding of P. gingivalis OMVs and to develop more effective preventive and therapeutic strategies for periodontal and related systemic diseases.

sec.title: Conflicts of interest
sec.p: None disclosed.
